Now, question number one. What really is so special about a straight line script? Essentially, why do they convert it to such a higher level than any other type of sales script out there? And I promise you that they most definitely do, almost shockingly so. And I've tested just about every other type of sales script you can imagine. I've done my research here, so trust me on this. So that being said, let's go through the five things that set straight line scripts apart from everything else out there. And of course, these five things are basically what you need to have in place for your script to be effective. So first and most important is your scripts need to be structured in a way that's formulaic. In other words, they need to have a basic structure or formula that can be used in any situation in any industry and for any outcome you want. Now I'm going to give you some very basic structures here, some simple rules and guidelines that will allow you to adapt the straight line scripting process to your specific industry. We'll get to that later. Now number two, the scripts must be written in a way that sounds totally natural and pleasing to the ear. And that is a hugely important quality to any script. Like I've said before, you can never sound like you're reading from a script, so you need to actually master the art of reading from a script without sounding like you're reading from a script. Okay, we'll get to that again later as well. Number three, straight line scripts are not what I call front loaded. Now what I mean by that is that you don't put every single feature and benefit right up front in the first few paragraphs. Essentially, you're not trying to blast the person right out of the bat and get into the super high level of certainty straight away. And the reason for this is that as human beings, we're not built in a way that allows us to go from zero to 100 instantly. Not even a race car when you go from zero to 100 miles an hour, you have to shift gears and each time you do shift gears, there's a temporary pause in your forward motion and the engine gets quiet for a second and then it lurches forward again. It's almost like the engine's catching its breath. Let me give you a sales related example. Very often we find ourselves in a situation where our prospect is only moderately certain about us or our product. So we use a language pattern as part of a script to make them feel more certain. But since they're still not at a 10, they give you another objection and you then loop back and you can get another language pattern to raise up the level of certainty. This can typically go on to three or four objections as you keep looping back, what you're doing is using language patterns designed to bring up to this absolute highest level of certainty, which is a 10. In other words, the way you increase someone's level of certainty is bit by bit, not all at once. And when you front load, meaning you put all that great stuff right up front, you have nothing powerful left to say to convince your prospect to change their mind when they come back with another rebuttal. So you're dead in the water. That's front loading. And if you want to be closing at the highest level, you need to avoid it at all costs. Okay? Now, number four, the next thing that sets straight line scripts apart from everything else is that each script is an integral part of an overall system. It's not just one standalone script. There might be, in fact, five or six different scripts that you have to go through to complete the entire process from A to Z from the open to the close. First you have your main qualifier and trip where you identify your client's needs, their desires, any pain points they might have. Then number two, you'll have your body of your presentation, which will end with you asking for the order. Okay? And then three, you have all your rebuttal scripts, which will have your language patterns that allow you to loop back again and again and again to build the three tens at the highest level, then lower their action threshold, and then end on any pain if that's necessary. So in the end, you can close anyone who's closeable. Now, the backbone of the straight line scripting system is that you need to create four or five very cohesive statements called language patterns that you can run about your product, about yourself, and about your company. And each of these patterns are designed to raise someone's level of certainty for one of the three tens, while at the same time, moving them down the straight line from the open to the close. Now, let me explain to you what I'm talking about here real quick. Remember visually, the straight line looks like this. So at this point right here, this is where your qualifying script would be, and this is where your main script would be, and this is where you'd be after you asked for the order for the first time, you're now dealing with objections, and you've started the process of looping, which you'll then repeat again and again and again as you continue to move your client down the straight line from the open to the close until they buy. Make sense? And remember at any point in the sale, you cannot do one of these three things. One, you cannot break rapport with your prospect, that's death. Two, you can't say something that's just entirely ridiculous because, A, that of course you'd fall out of rapport, also you lose massive credibility. And number three, you can't say things that take the person outside of the actual influencing conversation itself. In other words, going off into Pluto or Uranus. If you do any of those three things, it's going to dramatically reduce your closing rate. Now, remember you have those boundaries above and below the line here and here. Now if at any point you start deviating from your scripts and stop talking about the price of tea in China, things that aren't actually germane to the influencing situation, then you lose. Essentially, you prove to the prospect that you're not an expert and you dramatically reduce your chances of closing the sale. So again, you'll be using scripts from here all the way to here, in the beginning, in the middle, and at the end, and they're all equally important. And when they're used together in exactly the right way, meaning in the exact right order and said with the exact right tonality, you'll be at that point where you truly will be able to close anyone who's closeable. Now finally, the fifth thing that sets straight line scripts apart from any other script is that they're proven to work. And I mean in basically every industry out there. And just so you know, from my part, I'm a big believer that when you have something that works, you stick with it and you lock it in. And for you, knowing that your scripts are solid right out of the gate allows you to focus away from what you're actually saying and onto how you're actually saying it. And it also allows you to listen more effectively to what your client is saying back to you.
